title: Color Tools & Resources for Designers and Developers
description: Generators, pickers, accessibility checkers, and reference systems for building and testing color palettes.

entries.name: Coolors
entries.summary: A fast palette generator that produces five-color schemes; pressing the spacebar cycles new palettes and colors can be locked.
entries.significance: One of the most widely used palette generators; low-friction spacebar workflow made it a default starting point for designers.
key_facts: Generates palettes instantly via spacebar; individual swatches can be locked.
Can extract a palette from an uploaded photo.
Includes a built-in contrast checker and export options.

entries.name: Adobe Color
entries.summary: A color-wheel tool that builds harmonies (complementary, analogous, triadic, etc.) and includes accessibility and color-blindness checks.
entries.significance: Long-standing, authoritative wheel-based tool integrated with Creative Cloud libraries; strong for learning harmony rules.
key_facts: Builds schemes from harmony rules on an interactive color wheel.
Includes a contrast-ratio check and a color-blind-safe palette tool.
Palettes save to Creative Cloud libraries.

entries.name: Paletton
entries.summary: A color-wheel scheme designer for building mono, complementary, triadic, and tetradic palettes with live preview.
entries.significance: A long-running, education-friendly tool for exploring classic harmony relationships and previewing them on sample layouts.
key_facts: Supports monochromatic, adjacent (analogous), triad, and tetrad schemes.
Previews palettes on example web-page and artwork mockups.

entries.name: Colormind
entries.summary: An AI palette generator that uses deep learning trained on photos, movies, and popular art to produce cohesive five-color schemes.
entries.significance: An early example of ML-driven palette generation; learns context so locked colors influence the rest of the palette.
key_facts: Uses a neural network trained on images and existing palettes.
Can generate palettes conditioned on user-locked colors.
Offers a public API.

entries.name: Khroma
entries.summary: An AI color tool that first learns your taste from an initial color-selection step, then generates personalized combinations.
entries.significance: Notable for a personalization step: it trains on colors you like, then filters generated pairs to your preferences.
key_facts: User picks ~50 favorite colors to train a personal model.
Generates typographic, gradient, and image previews of color pairs.
Flags color pairs for contrast/accessibility.

entries.name: Realtime Colors
entries.summary: A tool that applies a palette to a realistic website mockup in real time so you can judge text, background, primary, secondary, and accent roles in context.
entries.significance: Shifted palette selection from abstract swatches to seeing colors on a real UI, including live dark/light toggling.
key_facts: Maps colors to semantic roles: text, background, primary, secondary, accent.
Previews the palette live on a full sample landing page.
Exports to CSS variables, Tailwind, and other formats.

entries.name: Huemint
entries.summary: An ML-based color generator aimed at brand and UI palettes, producing context-aware combinations for logos, websites, and graphics.
entries.significance: Uses a custom model to generate palettes tuned to specific layout contexts rather than generic five-swatch sets.
key_facts: Generates palettes for specific templates (brand, website, gradient, illustration).
Lets users lock colors and adjust a 'creativity/contrast' control.

entries.name: ColorBox
entries.summary: A tool for generating systematic color scales by defining curves for hue, saturation, and brightness across a number of steps.
entries.significance: Built by Lyft's design team to produce mathematically consistent tint/shade ramps for design systems rather than hand-picked swatches.
key_facts: Generates N-step scales from configurable hue/saturation/brightness curves.
Aimed at producing consistent, reproducible color ramps for UI systems.

entries.name: Leonardo
entries.summary: An open-source tool that generates color scales from target contrast ratios, so every step meets a chosen accessibility threshold.
entries.significance: Inverts the usual workflow — you specify desired contrast ratios and Leonardo computes the colors — making accessible-by-construction palettes practical.
key_facts: Generates colors from desired contrast ratios (e.g. every step meets 3:1 or 4.5:1).
Supports background-relative contrast and colorblind-safe cycling.
Open source on GitHub (adobe/leonardo).

entries.name: Radix Colors
entries.summary: An open-source color system of 12-step scales engineered for specific UI roles, with an in-browser custom-palette generator.
entries.significance: Provides accessible, role-mapped scales (backgrounds, borders, solids, text) that drop into design systems, plus a generator for custom brand scales.
key_facts: 12 steps per scale mapped to UI roles (bg 1-2, components 3-5, borders 6-8, solids 9-10, text 11-12).
Includes light, dark, and transparent variants.
The /custom generator builds an accessible 12-step scale from a brand color.

entries.name: Open Color
entries.summary: An open-source, optimized color scheme of 13 hues each in 10 numbered steps, tuned for UI use.
entries.significance: A free, no-dependency palette designed specifically for UI so developers can pull consistent, tested swatches without designing a ramp.
key_facts: 13 hues (gray plus 12 colors), each with 10 steps (0-9).
Distributed as CSS/SCSS/JSON variables.
Hand-tuned specifically for user interfaces.

entries.name: Happy Hues
entries.summary: A curated palette resource that demonstrates each palette applied to an actual example website, showing how to assign colors to roles.
entries.significance: Teaches color application (not just selection) by showing the same page rendered in many curated palettes with role labels.
key_facts: The site itself is the demo: switching palettes recolors the whole page live.
Labels which color to use for background, paragraph, buttons, etc.

entries.name: Color Hunt
entries.summary: A community-curated library of four-color palettes, browsable and sortable by popularity and trend.
entries.significance: A large, browsable gallery of ready-made palettes useful for quick inspiration rather than generation.
key_facts: Palettes are four colors each, submitted and voted on by the community.
Sortable by new, popular, and random.

entries.name: WebAIM Contrast Checker
entries.summary: A widely-trusted tool that computes the WCAG contrast ratio between two colors and reports AA/AAA pass/fail for normal and large text.
entries.significance: A de-facto reference for verifying WCAG 1.4.3 contrast; from WebAIM, a leading web-accessibility organization.
key_facts: Reports pass/fail against 4.5:1 (normal text) and 3:1 (large text) at AA, and 7:1/4.5:1 at AAA.
Large text is defined as 18pt / 14pt bold.
Includes lightness sliders to nudge colors into compliance.

entries.name: Stark
entries.summary: An accessibility toolkit (Figma/Sketch/browser plugins) that checks contrast, simulates color blindness, and audits designs against WCAG.
entries.significance: Brought accessibility checks directly into design tools, letting teams catch contrast issues before build.
key_facts: Runs contrast checks and colorblind simulations inside Figma and other tools.
Includes broader accessibility auditing beyond color.

entries.name: WhoCanUse
entries.summary: A contrast tool that frames results by who can actually use a color pairing, simulating various vision types and conditions.
entries.significance: Reframes contrast from an abstract ratio into human impact by estimating the share of people affected by common vision differences.
key_facts: Shows the color pair under many vision simulations (color blindness, low vision, cataracts, etc.).
Reports the WCAG ratio plus AA/AAA status.

entries.name: Accessible Colors
entries.summary: A checker that validates a text/background pair against WCAG AA and, if it fails, suggests the nearest compliant colors.
entries.significance: Useful for quickly nudging a failing pair to the closest passing colors while preserving hue.
key_facts: Checks against WCAG 2.0 AA for the given font size and weight.
Suggests adjusted foreground/background values that pass.

entries.name: Colorable
entries.summary: A tool that shows WCAG contrast scores for combinations of a set of colors, highlighting which foreground/background pairs pass.
entries.significance: Good for auditing an entire palette at once: it renders a matrix of pairings with their ratios and AA/AAA grades.
key_facts: Displays contrast ratio and AA/AAA pass grades for color combinations.
Open source; helps validate a palette's usable text pairings.

entries.name: Pantone Matching System (PMS)
entries.summary: A standardized spot-color matching system used to specify and reproduce exact colors consistently across print and manufacturing.
entries.significance: The industry standard for specifying brand colors physically; PMS numbers let vendors reproduce a precise ink regardless of screen rendering.
key_facts: Each color has a unique PMS number (e.g. Tiffany's custom '1837 Blue').
Guides come in coated (C), uncoated (U), and other finishes.
Used for spot inks where CMYK cannot reliably reproduce a color.

entries.name: uiGradients
entries.summary: A curated, community-driven collection of two-plus-color gradients with copyable CSS.
entries.significance: A quick source of ready gradients with exportable CSS, popular during the gradient-heavy UI era.
key_facts: Browse named gradients and copy the CSS linear-gradient.
Community-contributed and open source.

entries.name: CSS Gradient
entries.summary: A visual generator for linear and radial CSS gradients that outputs ready-to-paste code, including fallbacks.
entries.significance: Lowers the friction of hand-writing gradient syntax; useful for quick prototyping of gradient backgrounds.
key_facts: Generates linear and radial gradients with adjustable stops, angle, and position.
Outputs cross-browser CSS with a solid-color fallback.

entries.name: OKLCH Color Picker (Evil Martians)
entries.summary: A color picker built around the OKLCH model, showing lightness, chroma, and hue plus the P3/sRGB gamut boundary and contrast.
entries.significance: Helps designers reason in perceptually-uniform terms and see which OKLCH colors fall inside sRGB vs wide-gamut P3, supporting modern CSS color workflows.
key_facts: Edits colors as OKLCH lightness/chroma/hue.
Visualizes the sRGB and Display-P3 gamut limits and clipping.
Shows contrast information for accessibility.

sources.title: OK, OKLCH: a color picker made to help think perceptively
